title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Prices Maintain Momentum Post Peak Early in Week
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(18th November â€“ 24th November)
  • A total of 263,653 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 16.08% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a fall of 5.18% WoW as 403,653 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased this week to $76.25/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 8.56%Â rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 4.91% in price reaching $72.59/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price declined marginally by 0.03% WoW; on 26th Nov, it was $76.50/MT. The benchmark price stood at $76.75/MT on 26th November.
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 10.07% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each fell by 13.51% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

In the two weeks that have followed significant regulatory updates to the LCFS Program, spot prices have reached new peaks and have stabilized at a higher average. The week under observation started off to a strong start with spot prices recording a value of $78/MT on 18th November. On 15th November, spot prices had reached a high of $78.25, a price that the LCFS had last witnessed on 28th August 2023. While stakeholders wait in anticipation for the start of Q1 2025 when the amended LCFS Program is set to take effect, the market is abuzz with conversations on the impact of several aspects of the amendments. In particular, the implications of stricter feedstock requirements on Renewable Diesel output volumes have been an area of interest. Additionally, the future of Sustainable Aviation Fuel, book and claim accounting provisions for RNG to EV charging pathways and modifications to the crediting periods for avoided methane emissions in RNG pathways are aspects that would impact credit generation in the LCFS Program in the coming year.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB The average Type 1 prices recorded an 8.56% WoW rise from $70.24 to $76.25 this week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices rose by 4.91% compared to the previous week. Type 1 trades saw 263,653 credits exchanged, recording a rise of 16.08% WoW this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades had decreased by 5.18% WoW, to reach 403,653 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info) Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decline marginally by 0.03% WoW, reaching $76.63/MT. On 26th November, the average spot price was $76.50/MT, a fall of 1.14% from 19th November. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $76.75/MT as of 26th November.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB As per the data reported by CARB last week, 45 trades were reported in the market, with two high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $78.50 for one transaction of Type 1 at a volume of 5000 credits, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $60.50 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 50,000 credits, making it also the largest volume of transfers for the week. The smallest volume transfers of 504 credits of Type 1 category, were undertaken for price of $76.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB With a week left in the month of November, volumes traded have been significantly lower than the preceding month. While October witnessed record volumes of 6.4 MMT, volumes of 1.32MMT have been recorded by the third week of November. Average Type 1 prices for November stood at $71.52/MT, higher than the average of $65.93/MT observed in October.

Â CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 18th Â Â Nov â€“ 24th Nov, total VPT recorded a 6.01% rise (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 10.07% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices fell by 13.51% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Anaergia Awarded Contract to Operate and Maintain Rialto Bioenergy Solutions Facility in California Anaergia Inc. (â€œAnaergiaâ€, the â€œCompanyâ€, â€œweâ€, â€œusâ€, or â€œourâ€) (TSX: ANRG) announced today that its subsidiary, Anaergia Services LLC (â€œAnaergia Servicesâ€), has entered into a long-term Operations and Maintenance (â€œO&Mâ€) contract with Rialto Bioenergy Solutions LLC (â€œRBSâ€), an affiliate of Sevana Bioenergy LLC. The RBS facility (â€œFacilityâ€) is the largest landfill diverted organic waste-to-renewable natural gas facility in North America.
  • RFA Welcomes National Energy Council, Doug Burgum as Chairman President-elect Donald Trump has announced that North Dakota Gov. Doug Burgum, in addition to joining his administration as Interior Secretary, will chair the new National Energy Council. The following is a statement from Renewable Fuels Association President and CEO Geoff Cooper. â€œWe applaud the formation of the National Energy Council and the appointment of Governor Burgum as its chairman. He fully understands the importance of adopting an all-of-the-above energy strategy that includes renewable fuels, carbon capture and sequestration, and other innovative approaches to energy and economic security. We look forward to working with him in this new capacity. For far too long, federal agencies have operated in siloes when it comes to energy policy and regulationâ€”we are excited by the prospect of bringing better coordination and collaboration to these important issues.â€
  • Vision RNG Announces Renewable Natural Gas Agreement at Waste Connections Landfill in Nebraska Vision RNG LLC has signed a 20-year renewable natural gas (RNG) agreement with L.P. Gill, Inc., a subsidiary of Waste Connections, Inc. The Jackson, Nebraska project will initially convert 1,200 cubic feet per minute of landfill gas into 310,000 MMBtu of RNG annually, with production expected to grow over the life of the contract. The RNG will be injected into a nearby pipeline and used across the U.S. for transportation fuel and other sustainable applications.
  • Rule Announcement Notice: Chapter 173-423 WAC, Clean Vehicles Program Rule Ecology is considering amendments to Chapter 173-423 WAC, the Clean Vehicles Program rule. Washington law (RCW 70A.30.010) requires Ecology to adopt certain California regulations to maintain consistency with Californiaâ€™s emissions standards for new motor vehicles and engines. To date, Ecology has delivered on this Legislative requirement to adopt regulations consistent with the following California programs: Advanced Clean Cars, Advanced Clean Cars II, Advanced Clean Trucks, and Heavy-Duty Low NOx Omnibus standards.
  • CARBâ€™s Updated Incentive Plan Focuses on Getting Small Businesses and Fleets into Zero-Emissions Technology The California Air Resources Board (CARB) approved a $34.94 million incentives funding plan that will continue support ongoing efforts to increase access to medium- and heavy-duty zero-emission trucks, buses, and equipment, with a focus on small businesses. The 2024-25 Funding Plan for Clean Transportation Incentives increases funding for small businesses that want to modernize their fleets with cleaner vehicles, including assistance for rentals, leases and truck loans.
  • Growth Energy, CFAA File New Opening Brief in Long-Stayed RVO Case On Wednesday, November 20th, Growth Energy, along with Clean Fuels Alliance America, filed a new opening brief with the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuitâ€¯(Case No. 20-1046) challenging the Environmental Protection Agencyâ€™s (EPA) failure to fully account for small refinery exemptions (SREs) when issuing renewable volume obligations (RVO) under the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS).

author: Aakanksha
